target_users.name: Weekend Sports Fan
target_users.description: A casual Irish sports enthusiast who wants to quickly find out what's on TV this weekend. They check on their phone (often on the couch) or desktop. Not particularly tech-savvy — they just want answers fast.
goals: Find out what sport is on TV this Saturday/Sunday
Know the exact start time in Irish time
Know which channel or streaming service to tune into
Quickly scan across multiple sports without clicking through multiple sites
pain_points: Information scattered across RTÉ, Sky, Virgin Media, and streaming apps
TV guides are cluttered with non-sport content
Hard to compare across sports at a glance

target_users.name: Sport-Specific Fan
target_users.description: A fan who follows one or two sports closely (e.g., GAA and rugby). They want to filter down to just their sport and ignore the rest.
goals: Filter fixtures to only the sports they care about
See all upcoming matches for their sport in one view
Quickly identify marquee fixtures (e.g., All-Ireland, Six Nations)
pain_points: Generic sports pages bury their sport under soccer headlines
No single place that covers GAA, rugby, and horse racing equally
Filtering is often absent or broken on mobile

target_users.name: Group Planner
target_users.description: Someone organising a viewing session with friends or family. They need to see the full weekend schedule to plan around multiple events.
goals: See the full weekend timeline at a glance
Identify clashes between fixtures on different channels
Share or screenshot the schedule easily
pain_points: Hard to see temporal overlaps across sports
Need to cross-reference multiple sources to build a plan

key_flows.name: Default Weekend View
steps: User opens the HTML file in their browser
Page loads with weekend fixtures (Saturday & Sunday) displayed by default
Fixtures are grouped by day, sorted chronologically
Each fixture card shows: sport icon/label, event name, date/time (IST), and TV channel/streaming service
On-page instructions banner is visible at the top explaining how to use filters
key_flows.notes: This is the zero-click experience. The most common use case should require no interaction at all.

key_flows.name: Filter by Sport
steps: User sees sport filter buttons/pills (Rugby, Soccer, GAA, Golf, Horse Racing) near the top
User taps/clicks one or more sport filters to toggle them on/off
Fixture list updates instantly (client-side) to show only selected sports
An 'All Sports' option resets filters
Active filters are visually highlighted
key_flows.notes: Filtering must be instant with no page reload. Toggle behavior (not dropdown) is preferred for discoverability and speed.

key_flows.name: Filter by Day
steps: User sees day filter options (Saturday, Sunday, or All Weekend)
User selects a day to narrow the fixture list
List updates immediately to show only that day's fixtures
key_flows.notes: Day filter works in combination with sport filter. Both filters are independently toggleable.

key_flows.name: View Fixture Details
steps: User scans the fixture list
Each entry clearly displays sport type, match/event name, kick-off/start time, and broadcast channel
Channel names use recognisable branding (e.g., RTÉ 2, Sky Sports, GAAGO, Virgin Media, Racing TV)
key_flows.notes: No expand/collapse or detail modals needed in v1.0. All info is visible inline on each card.

key_flows.name: Mobile Browse
steps: User opens the page on a mobile browser
Layout adapts to single-column card view
Filters stack horizontally and scroll if needed
Fixture cards are full-width and thumb-friendly
All text remains readable without zooming
key_flows.notes: Mobile is likely the primary device. Touch targets must be at least 44px.

ui_patterns: Card-based fixture layout: each fixture is a discrete card with sport label, event name, time, and channel
Pill/chip filter buttons for sport categories — visually distinct, toggle on/off with active state colour
Day selector as segmented control (Saturday | Sunday | All Weekend) above the fixture list
Chronological grouping with sticky day headers (e.g., 'Saturday 28 March') that persist while scrolling
Sport colour coding: assign a distinct accent colour to each sport (e.g., green for GAA, blue for rugby) for instant visual scanning
Channel/service badge: display broadcast info as a styled badge/tag within each card for quick recognition
Instruction banner: a subtle, dismissable help section at the top of the page explaining filters and layout
Empty state: if filters result in no fixtures, show a friendly message like 'No fixtures match your filters — try selecting more sports'
Single-column layout on mobile, two-column grid on tablet, three-column on desktop
No modals, no navigation — everything is on one scrollable page
accessibility_requirements: All interactive elements (filter buttons, links) are keyboard-navigable with visible focus indicators
Filter toggle state is conveyed via aria-pressed attribute on buttons
Sport colour coding is supplemented with text labels and icons — never colour-alone to convey meaning
Sufficient colour contrast ratios (WCAG 2.1 AA minimum: 4.5:1 for text, 3:1 for UI components)
Semantic HTML: use heading hierarchy (h1 for page title, h2 for day groups), lists or grid roles for fixture collections
Screen reader announcements when filter results update (aria-live region on the fixture list)
Readable default font size (minimum 16px body text) with no reliance on browser zoom
designer_notes: This is a simple, single-purpose tool — resist feature creep. The design should feel like a clean sports schedule poster, not a web app. Key principles: (1) Zero-click value — the default view should answer 'what's on this weekend?' immediately. (2) Scannability — users will visually scan, not read; use colour, typography hierarchy, and whitespace to make sport/time/channel pop. (3) Mobile-first — assume most users are on phones, design the card layout for 375px width first, then scale up. (4) Irish context — use familiar channel names (RTÉ, TG4, Sky Sports, Virgin Media, GAAGO, Racing TV) and sport terminology ('throw-in' not 'kick-off' for GAA). (5) Static data — since fixtures are hardcoded, include a visible 'Last updated' date so users know the data freshness. (6) The colour palette should feel sporty and energetic but not garish — consider a dark header with white/light card backgrounds. Green accents nod to Ireland without being heavy-handed. (7) All CSS and JS must be inline in the single HTML file — no CDN links, no external fonts. Use system font stack (e.g., -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', sans-serif) for zero-load-time typography.
